Policy Name/Number: EO 14319 – Preventing Woke AI in the Federal Government
Summary: Directs that AI systems used or procured by the federal government be "ideologically neutral" and not promote specified concepts, and tasks agencies and the Office of Management and Budget with aligning acquisition, testing, and reporting to those principles. Establishes definitions, assigns agency duties, and requires attestations/assurances from vendors supplying AI systems to the government.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to HHS, VA, and other health agencies that deploy or procure AI (e.g., chatbots, decision support, triage). May constrain equity-focused AI features (e.g., bias-mitigation or inclusive design) in federal systems and require vendor attestations on neutrality; contracts and 'Authorization to Operate' packages will need updated compliance language.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Policy Name/Number: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance (Section 504)
Summary: Finalizes the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services update to Section 504 regulations, strengthening requirements for effective communication, medical treatment non‑discrimination, web and mobile accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA), and protections in value‑assessment methods. Clarifies responsibilities for recipients of HHS funding and coordinates with related civil‑rights frameworks.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare providers, plans, and grantees must ensure accessibility across digital tools, clinical communications, and facility operations. AI‑mediated services (bots, decision support) must not result in disability discrimination and should follow accessibility standards and human‑in‑the‑loop practices aligned with Section 504 and related §1557 protections.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance
Summary: Finalizes disability nondiscrimination requirements for programs receiving federal financial assistance, including effective communication and web/mobile accessibility.
Healthcare Implications: Covered health entities must ensure accessibility; the Office of Civil Rights notes risks from automated decision‑making, complementing §1557 protections.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M‑24‑10 – Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of AI
Summary: Sets government‑wide AI governance requirements, including designating Chief AI Officers, maintaining AI use inventories, implementing risk management practices for rights- and safety‑impacting AI, and establishing procurement and testing standards.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, the Department of Veteran Affairs, the Department of Defense health programs, and contractors integrating AI into public‑facing or rights‑impacting services. Drives inventories, impact assessments, and assurance processes that affect clinical decision support, benefits eligibility automation, and health IT procurements.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: EO 14120 – Advancing Women's Health Research and Innovation
Summary: Launches a federal initiative to accelerate women’s health research and translation, coordinating across agencies and prioritizing funding, data, and innovation activities. Calls for closing evidence gaps and mobilizing cross‑sector collaboration.
Healthcare Implications: Encourages AI‑enabled research and diagnostic development specific to women’s health (e.g., imaging, cardiology, maternal outcomes). Signals funding and data priorities relevant to health systems, researchers, and AI developers.
Keyword Tags: Privacy & Data; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: EO 14117 – Preventing Access to Americans’ Bulk Sensitive Personal Data and U.S. Government‑Related Data by Countries of Concern
Summary: Addresses national‑security risks from transfers of bulk sensitive personal data (including health, genomic, biometric, precise geolocation) to countries of concern. Directs the Department of Justice and other agencies to propose regulations restricting certain transactions and establishing safeguards.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems, data brokers, and app developers handling health/genomic data must evaluate cross‑border data flows and third‑party relationships. AI training and analytics that use sensitive datasets will need vendor due diligence and technical/contractual controls to avoid prohibited transfers.

Policy Name/Number: HTI-1 – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: Certification Program Updates, Algorithm Transparency, and Information Sharing
Summary: Updates the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology Health IT Certification Program, adopting the Decision Support Interventions certification criterion with new transparency for predictive models; adopts USCDI v3; moves to an "edition‑less" certification approach; and updates information blocking provisions and technical standards. Includes an "Insights Condition and Maintenance of Certification" to collect aggregated performance data from certified developers.
Healthcare Implications: Directly impacts electronic health records and certified health IT used by providers: developers must disclose key characteristics of predictive decision support interventions (e.g., intended use, training data, risk management), improving transparency for clinicians, patients, and implementers. Supports safer AI integration and interoperability across care settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: EO 14110 – Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directed a whole‑of‑government approach to AI safety, security, and equity, including safety test reporting for powerful models, standards development at the National Institute of Standards and Technology, infrastructure security, and civil‑rights protections. Provided agency tasks on healthcare, workforce, and R&D.
Healthcare Implications: Spurred the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services and other agencies to evaluate risks in clinical algorithms, bias in health programs, and AI safety testing. Note: EO 14110 was revoked by EO 14179 (Jan. 23, 2025); agencies were instructed to review and modify actions taken under 14110. Some derivative guidance (e.g., OMB M‑24‑10) remains in effect unless separately rescinded.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: National Artificial Intelligence Research and Development Strategic Plan – 2023 Update
Summary: Sets federal priorities across nine strategic areas for AI research, including trustworthy AI, safety, privacy‑preserving techniques, human‑AI collaboration, and evaluation; calls for robust testbeds, datasets, and workforce development. Guides multi‑agency R&D coordination and investment.
Healthcare Implications: Identifies research thrusts directly relevant to health AI – privacy‑preserving learning, robustness, evaluation benchmarks, and human‑centered design. Shapes the National Institute of Health's, the National Science Foundation's, and agency grantmaking that providers and developers can leverage for clinical AI.

Policy Name/Number: EO 14091 – Further Advancing Racial Equity and Support for Underserved Communities Through the Federal Government
Summary: Builds on EO 13985 to embed equity across federal programs, including establishing agency equity teams, improving data collection, and addressing discrimination risks in automated technology. Sets annual agency action plans and accountability structures.
Healthcare Implications: Reinforces civil‑rights expectations for health programs and federal contractors, including attention to algorithmic bias in benefits and service delivery. Supports equitable access initiatives and data practices relevant to AI tools used by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services and health grantees.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Public Law 117-207 (SB2551) – Artificial Intelligence Training for the Acquisition Workforce Act
Summary: Directs the Office of Management and Budget to establish AI training for the federal acquisition workforce, covering AI benefits, risks, ethics, and procurement best practices; requires agency participation and updates.
Healthcare Implications: Elevates due diligence for AI-enabled health IT procurements across federal health agencies (HHS, VA), impacting vendor requirements for explainability, bias mitigation, and data rights.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
Summary: Outlines five principles for safe, effective, and rights‑preserving automated systems: safe and effective systems; algorithmic discrimination protections; data privacy; notice and explanation; and human alternatives/consideration/fallback. Accompanied by a technical companion and examples to guide implementation.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a policy baseline for health organizations building/using AI: emphasize safety/effectiveness validation, discrimination testing, privacy by design for sensitive health data, clear patient/provider notices, and human‑in‑the‑loop options for consequential decisions.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: Advancing Biotechnology and Biomanufacturing Innovation for a Sustainable, Safe, and Secure American Bioeconomy
Summary: Directs a whole‑of‑government strategy to advance biotechnology and biomanufacturing via R&D, domestic capacity, biosafety/biosecurity, and data infrastructure; requires agency plans and reports.
Healthcare Implications: Connects AI with biological data analysis, forecasting, and design; encourages AI‑enabled discovery in genomics, therapeutics, and bioproduction.

Policy Name/Number: EO 13859 – Maintaining American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Launches the American AI Initiative, prioritizing R&D, data access, workforce development, and international engagement to sustain U.S. AI leadership. Directs agencies to prioritize AI in budgets and policy coordination.
Healthcare Implications: Catalyzed agency AI strategies and research initiatives relevant to health AI (NIH, FDA, HHS). Framed open data and R&D priorities that supported clinical AI research and the health‑AI workforce.
